Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative fixed clause with prepositional phrase functioning metaphorically
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: News or gossip spreads very quickly within the community
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: El noticia ya circula rapidamente entre la gente
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects coastal and wind-centered imagery common in Zamboanga maritime culture
First Example Sentence: Anda na hangin el noticia que ta subi el precio del pescado na mercado.
Second Example Sentence: Antes pa llega el anuncio oficial, anda ya na hangin el noticia.
Third Example Sentence: Si tiene secreto, no confiá, porque anda na hangin el noticia aqui.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Descriptive comparative clause with simile structure
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who behaves differently depending on the situation; hypocritical
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ele ta cambia actitud depende con quien ele ta conversa
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from Spanish metaphor comparing duplicity to two-sided coin imagery
First Example Sentence: No confiá con ele porque tiene dos cara como moneda.
Second Example Sentence: Na reunion amable ele, pero afuera tiene dos cara como moneda.
Third Example Sentence: El gente ya cansa con su actitud que tiene dos cara como moneda.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Resultative stative construction with locative complement
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be left in an awkward or uncertain situation
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Queda ele na situacion dificil y incierto
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From Spanish 'alanganín' used in Philippine Spanish dialects to indicate hesitation or uncertainty
First Example Sentence: Ya queda yo na alanganin cuando no sila ya llega.
Second Example Sentence: Si no tu termina el proyecto, queda kita na alanganin con el jefe.
Third Example Sentence: El plan ya falla y ahora queda sila na alanganin.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal phrase functioning as evaluative comment
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ele ta habla mucho pero no ta actua
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Blend of Spanish 'palabra' with Philippine discourse particle 'lang'
First Example Sentence: Ese promesa suyo puro palabra lang.
Second Example Sentence: No yo cree con ele porque puro palabra lang siempre.
Third Example Sentence: Ya demuestra ya que no puro palabra lang su intención.

Idiom (Chavacano): Ta carga el mundo na hombro
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Progressive verbal clause with direct object metaphor
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To carry heavy responsibilities or emotional burden
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ele tiene demasiado responsabilidad y preocupacion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes biblical and Hispanic imagery of burden-bearing figures
First Example Sentence: Desde que ya enferma su tata, ta carga el mundo na hombro ele.
Second Example Sentence: Mira ele como si ta carga el mundo na hombro todo el dia.
Third Example Sentence: No necesita tu ta carga el mundo na hombro solo.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed nominal clause with reduplication and evaluative particle
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A fair fight or equal competition
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Igual el oportunidad entre dos parte
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Filipino reduplication influence blended with Spanish lexicon
First Example Sentence: Na torneo, mano-mano lang el laban entre dos equipo.
Second Example Sentence: Quiere ele que mano-mano lang el laban sin ventaja.
Third Example Sentence: Si justo el reglas, mano-mano lang el laban.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To destroy a relationship or eliminate possibility of return
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ele ya rompe completamente el relacion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from Spanish military metaphor of burning bridges to prevent retreat
First Example Sentence: Cuando ya insulta ele con su jefe, quema ya el puente.
Second Example Sentence: No tu quema el puente si necesita pa tu ese conexion.
Third Example Sentence: Por orgullo, ya quema ele el puente con su familia.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Intransitive verbal clause with locative complement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Hispanic physiological metaphor linking anger to rising blood
First Example Sentence: Ya sube el sangre na cabeza cuando ya mira ele el daño.
Second Example Sentence: No provoca conmigo porque facil sube el sangre na cabeza.
Third Example Sentence: Por el injusticia, sube el sangre na cabeza del gente.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Progressive verbal clause with prepositional complement
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To change allegiance or follow a different agenda
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ele ya cambia posicion o grupo
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Music-based metaphor common in Hispanic cultures indicating shifting loyalty
First Example Sentence: Antes aliado ele, pero ahora ta baila con otro son.
Second Example Sentence: El lider ya baila con otro son despues del eleccion.
Third Example Sentence: No claro su postura porque siempre ta baila con otro son.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Passive-like verbal clause with locative phrase
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Advice or warning is ignored completely
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: El consejo no ya escucha ni considera
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From Spanish 'caer en oídos sordos' adapted into Chavacano phonology
First Example Sentence: Todo su advertencia ya cae na oido sordo.
Second Example Sentence: Si no tu presta atencion, cae na oido sordo el consejo.
Third Example Sentence: El llamada del comunidad ya cae na oido sordo con autoridad.
